preFix.title: Whittled ash dibber, short
preFix.subtitle: A 20 cm ash dibber for seed trays and potting compost
preFix.excerpt: A short-handled ash dibber for making planting holes in seed trays and potting compost. The pointed tip is whittled from a green ash twig; the project takes under an hour and makes a useful tool.
preFix.sourceNotes: Cassell's Cyclopaedia of Mechanics vol. III (1885) for the tool-handle whittling method; Beeton's Book of Household Management (1861) for the household gardening implement forms.

content.text: A short dibber is used for seed trays and 9 cm pots: small enough holes at close spacing, with control over depth. At 20 cm long and 12 mm in diameter, an ash twig makes the right size with minimal work. Ash is the recommended species because it is straight-grained in small branches and hardens well as it dries, which means the dibber point survives seasons of pushing into compacted soil.
Green wood
whittles most cleanly; the dried piece is durable.
Wood

Cut a straight ash twig 22 cm long and 14 to 16 mm in diameter. Ash is hard enough to hold a point in garden use and does not splinter dangerously when it eventually wears. Hazel is a close alternative: slightly lighter, equally durable as a garden tool. Remove the bark with the sloyd knife along the handle section; leave the bark on the lower 6 cm that will be buried in compost if you want a natural look, or peel it all for a uniform piece.
Tools
Sloyd knife: for bark removal and point shaping.
Leather strop: strop every fifteen minutes.
Before you start cutting.
Grip: four-finger grip on the work; thumb-push grip on the knife. Never make a cut whose blade-path can reach a body part.
Cut direction: push cuts (blade away from body) and thumb-pivot cuts are the default. Pull cuts only with the work braced against the chest and the blade-arc unable to reach anything vital.
Sharpening: a dull tool is more dangerous than a sharp one. Strop every fifteen minutes of carving, hone weekly, oilstone monthly.
First aid: keep a styptic pencil, gauze, and a clean dressing within reach. Apply firm pressure, elevate, and dress. A cut that shows fat or will not close with pressure goes to A&E, not a plaster.
Workspace: stable seat, good light, no children or pets within arm-and-blade reach.
Removing the bark
Hold the twig in the four-finger grip. Use
push cuts

along the handle, blade travelling from the grip end toward the point end. The blade rides just under the bark. Ash bark in spring and early summer separates easily; in autumn the bark is tighter and needs more knife pressure.
Shaping the point
The dibber point starts 6 cm from the bottom of the twig. Mark the start of the taper with a light
stop cut

around the circumference: a ring cut that will prevent the taper from running up into the handle section. Then take push cuts from the stop-cut line down toward the point. Each cut removes a thin shaving, reducing the diameter from 12 mm to a blunt tip 4 mm across. A sharp point is not needed and would snap on first use; a blunt-pointed cone makes the correct plug of soil.

Work the taper evenly by rotating the twig one quarter-turn between each series of cuts. Check the taper by holding the dibber at arm's length: if one side of the cone is fuller than another, take two or three cuts from the fuller side.
The handle

The handle section stays at the full twig diameter, 12 to 14 mm. Round off any rough patches from the bark removal with light push cuts. The handle end can be left squared or rounded to a dome; a rounded end is more comfortable on the palm when pressing the dibber into dense compost.
Finishing

This is a garden tool, not a food-contact item. No oil finish is needed. Leave the dibber to dry at room temperature for one week. The dried ash will lighten to a pale grey-white. A light coat of raw linseed oil is optional if you want to slow weathering; it is not required for function.
Variations
Depth marks: cut shallow ring grooves at 20, 30, and 40 mm from the tip with the sloyd knife. These act as depth guides when planting at consistent depths.
Long version: a 35 cm twig at 16 mm diameter makes a dibber for outdoor bed work and transplanting.
Hazel: cut from last year's coppice growth; hazel's pale wood makes the depth marks highly visible against the white surface.
Care
Rinse off soil after use and dry before storing. Re-whittle the point if it rounds off with use; the whole reshaping takes two minutes.
Troubleshooter

attrs.fix: A dibber tip should be 4 to 5 mm across at the end. If the tip snapped, re-whittle the remaining taper to a new blunt tip.
attrs.cause: Point too sharp and too thin
attrs.symptom: The point broke off on first use

attrs.fix: The ring stop cut is the key step. Without it, the grain will split along the length. If a split has started, keep it dry and bind the handle section with linen thread soaked in linseed to stabilise it.
attrs.cause: Push cuts went past the stop cut or no stop cut was made
attrs.symptom: The taper started a split up the handle

attrs.fix: Leave the bark on the handle section, or add shallow diagonal grip channels with the knife tip before drying.
attrs.cause: Bark entirely removed and wood too smooth
attrs.symptom: The handle feels slippery in use

initialFindings.message: PATTERN tutorial has no Method/Steps section and no orderedList — sequential instructions missing
